questions.text: What is the core difference between push-based and pull-based CD?

options.text: Push-based uses Helm; pull-based uses Kustomize
In pull-based, an agent in the cluster pulls desired state from Git; in push-based, a CI system pushes to the cluster
Pull-based requires manual approval; push-based is fully automated
They are functionally identical

questions.explanation: In pull-based GitOps (ArgoCD, Flux), an agent running inside the cluster continuously polls Git and pulls changes. This means the cluster doesn't need to be reachable from CI, improving security. Push-based (like kubectl in CI) requires CI to have cluster credentials.

questions.text: In ArgoCD, what does 'prune: true' in syncPolicy.automated do?

options.text: Deletes old ArgoCD application revisions
Removes Kubernetes resources that exist in the cluster but are no longer in Git
Prunes unused container images
Compresses the Git repository

questions.explanation: When prune: true is set, ArgoCD deletes cluster resources that have been removed from the Git repository. Without this, ArgoCD won't delete resources even if you remove them from Git — the cluster would have orphaned resources.

options.text: Restarts crashed pods automatically
Reverts manual changes made directly to the cluster back to the Git state
Automatically fixes linting errors in Git
Rolls back failed deployments

questions.explanation: selfHeal: true tells ArgoCD to detect and fix drift. If someone manually edits a Deployment or ConfigMap in the cluster (bypassing Git), ArgoCD will re-sync and revert those changes back to what's in Git. This enforces Git as the single source of truth.

questions.text: In Flux v2, what resource type defines a Git repository source?

options.text: FluxSource
GitSync
GitRepository
SourceRepository

questions.explanation: In Flux v2, a GitRepository resource (source.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v1) defines the Git repo, branch/tag/commit to watch, and poll interval. Kustomization and HelmRelease resources then reference it via sourceRef to get their manifests.

questions.text: What is a Canary deployment in the context of Argo Rollouts?

options.text: Deploying to a separate canary cluster
Gradually routing a percentage of traffic to a new version while keeping the old version running
A deployment that only runs during off-peak hours
A blue-green switch with instant traffic cutover

questions.explanation: Canary deployments route a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 10%) to the new version, monitor metrics, then gradually increase the percentage. Argo Rollouts automates this with traffic management (Nginx, Istio, etc.) and analysis templates for auto-promotion or rollback.

questions.text: In ArgoCD, what is an 'App of Apps' pattern?

options.text: A single ArgoCD Application that deploys multiple microservices
An ArgoCD Application whose Git source contains other ArgoCD Application manifests
A Helm chart that contains multiple subcharts
An ApplicationSet that uses a cluster generator

questions.explanation: The App of Apps pattern uses a root ArgoCD Application that points to a Git directory containing other Application manifests. When synced, it creates all child Applications. This allows managing an entire platform's GitOps config declaratively in Git.

questions.text: What does Flux's 'interval' field on a GitRepository control?

options.text: How often Flux commits to Git
How often Flux polls the Git repository for changes
The timeout for kubectl apply operations
The reconciliation interval for Helm releases only

questions.explanation: The interval field (e.g., '1m', '5m') controls how frequently Flux polls the Git repository for new commits. Shorter intervals mean faster deployments but more API calls. Flux also supports webhook receivers for event-driven reconciliation without polling.

questions.text: What is the main security advantage of pull-based GitOps over push-based CD?

options.text: All manifests are encrypted in Git
The cluster doesn't need to be directly reachable from the CI system — no outbound cluster credentials in CI
Pull-based uses mTLS; push-based uses basic auth
Git webhooks are more secure than CI tokens

questions.explanation: In pull-based GitOps, CI never directly accesses the cluster — it only pushes to Git. The in-cluster GitOps agent (ArgoCD/Flux) then pulls from Git. This means cluster credentials don't need to be stored in CI systems, reducing the attack surface.

options.text: A group of Argo Workflows
A controller that generates multiple ArgoCD Applications from templates and generators
A set of Helm values for multiple environments
A Kustomize overlay set

questions.explanation: ApplicationSet uses generators (List, Cluster, Git directory, Pull Request) to automatically create and manage multiple ArgoCD Applications from a template. For example, a cluster generator can deploy an app to every registered cluster without manually creating an Application per cluster.

questions.text: How does Sealed Secrets solve secret management in GitOps?

options.text: Stores secrets in a separate private Git repo
Encrypts secrets with a cluster-specific key so only that cluster can decrypt them — safe to commit to Git
Replaces secrets with references to AWS Secrets Manager
Uses SOPS to encrypt all YAML files

questions.explanation: Sealed Secrets (by Bitnami) uses asymmetric encryption: you encrypt a Secret with the cluster's public key (creating a SealedSecret). Only the in-cluster controller with the private key can decrypt it. SealedSecrets are safe to commit to public Git repos.
